hero.tagline: The nervous system - turning plans into actual motion
hero.intro: A planner says 'go to (x, y) heading north'. A controller is what makes that actually happen, fighting friction, gravity, motor delays, and noisy sensors 1000 times a second. Get controllers right and the robot looks elegant; get them wrong and it shakes, overshoots, or crashes. This branch starts at the most useful three-letter algorithm in the world (PID), then moves up to state-space methods, model predictive control, force / impedance control for contact, real-time scheduling, and the ros2_control framework that wraps it all up. If you only learn one branch, learn this one.

sections.heading: Feedback loops - the core idea
sections.body: A controller closes the loop: measure the system's current state, subtract from the desired state to get error, compute a corrective command, apply it through actuators, and measure again. This loop runs at the controller's rate - typically 100 Hz for arms / mobile robots, 500-1000 Hz for inner attitude loops on drones, 10-50 Hz for path-following on cars. The hardest concept: stability - the loop must dampen errors over time, not amplify them. Bode plots, root locus, and Lyapunov analysis are the classical tools.
bullets: Open-loop = command without measurement; brittle, non-robust to disturbances
Closed-loop = command computed from error; robust, can be unstable if mistuned
Stability - necessary precondition; controller must dampen any disturbance over time
Bandwidth = how fast the loop can respond; limited by sensor noise, actuator delay, sample rate
Disturbance rejection - how well the loop cancels external pushes (wind, friction, payload)
Steady-state error - what error remains after the loop has settled (integral term kills it)

sections.heading: PID - the universal starting point
sections.body: PID - Proportional + Integral + Derivative - is the most widely deployed feedback controller on the planet. The math: u = Kp·e + Ki·∫e dt + Kd·de/dt, where e is the error. Tune the widget below: high Kp gives fast but oscillatory response; high Kd damps but introduces noise sensitivity; Ki kills steady-state error but can wind up if the actuator saturates. PID is the baseline against which every fancier controller is judged - and surprisingly often, the fancier controller doesn't win.
bullets: P term - pulls toward setpoint; too high causes oscillation
I term - eliminates steady-state error; needs anti-windup when actuator saturates
D term - damps the rate of change; amplifies sensor noise (often add a filter)
Tuning: Ziegler-Nichols (oscillation method), manual loop-shaping, Bayesian optimization
Anti-windup: clamp integrator when output saturates (back-calculation, conditional integration)
Cascaded PIDs: outer position loop → inner velocity loop → innermost current loop. Each loop ~10× faster than its outer

codeBlocks.title: Discrete PID with anti-windup (Python)
Same PID in real-time C++ (no allocations)

sections.heading: State-space + LQR - when PID isn't enough
sections.body: Above PID sits state-space control. The system is described as ẋ = Ax + Bu (state derivative as a linear function of current state and control input). The controller is u = -Kx, where K is computed to minimize a quadratic cost J = ∫(xᵀQx + uᵀRu) dt - this is the Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR). Q penalizes state error, R penalizes control effort - tune the trade-off and the optimal K falls out of an algebraic Riccati equation. Powerful for multi-input multi-output (MIMO) systems where PID's single-loop assumption breaks.
bullets: State-space form: ẋ = Ax + Bu, y = Cx + Du. Captures any linear system
Controllability: can you steer from any state to any other? (Kalman rank test)
LQR: optimal full-state feedback gain K minimizing ∫(xᵀQx + uᵀRu)
Observer / Kalman filter: estimate x from noisy y when full state isn't measured
Pole placement: alternative to LQR - pick desired closed-loop poles, solve for K
Robust control (H∞) - design for the worst-case model uncertainty

codeBlocks.title: Solve LQR with python-control
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np
import control as ct

# Example: cart-pole linearised about upright
A = np.array([[0, 1, 0,    0],
              [0, 0, -2.45, 0],
              [0, 0, 0,    1],
              [0, 0, 31.5, 0]])
B = np.array([[0], [0.95], [0], [-1.43]])
Q = np.diag([10, 1, 100, 1])
R = np.array([[0.1]])

K, S, E = ct.lqr(A, B, Q, R)
print('LQR gain K =', K)

sections.heading: Model Predictive Control (MPC)
sections.body: MPC is the most widely deployed advanced controller. At each timestep: predict the system's behavior over a horizon (e.g. 1-2 seconds at 50 Hz = 50-100 steps), formulate a quadratic program that finds the input sequence minimizing tracking error subject to constraints (joint limits, force limits, obstacle avoidance), apply the first input, then re-plan next step. Handles constraints natively - that's why it dominates wherever physical limits matter (drone aggressive flight, autonomous-vehicle planning, manipulation in clutter).
bullets: Horizon - number of steps the controller predicts ahead. 10-100 typical
Cost function: tracking + control effort + soft constraint penalties
Hard constraints: joint limits, actuator limits, obstacle clearance

Solvers: acados (the production default in 2026, generates C code, runs on micro), HPIPM (the QP underneath acados), OSQP (sparse QP, Python-friendly), ProxQP (modern QP, easy Python API), CVXGEN, IPOPT (large NLPs)
Update rate: must solve in less than 1 / horizon-resolution seconds - hard constraint
Variants: linear MPC (fast), nonlinear MPC (slower, exact), iLQR (sequential linearization)

codeBlocks.title: Tiny MPC sketch with cvxpy
codeBlocks.code:
import cvxpy as cp
import numpy as np

N = 20                  # horizon
A = np.array([[1, 1], [0, 1]])
B = np.array([[0], [1]])
Q = np.eye(2); R = np.array([[0.1]])

x0 = np.array([5.0, 0.0])   # initial state

x = cp.Variable((2, N + 1))
u = cp.Variable((1, N))
cost = 0; constraints = [x[:, 0] == x0]
for k in range(N):
    cost += cp.quad_form(x[:, k], Q) + cp.quad_form(u[:, k], R)
    constraints += [x[:, k + 1] == A @ x[:, k] + B @ u[:, k]]
    constraints += [cp.abs(u[:, k]) <= 2.0]
prob = cp.Problem(cp.Minimize(cost), constraints)
prob.solve()
# Apply only u[:, 0], then re-plan next step (receding horizon)

sections.heading: Force, impedance, admittance control
sections.body: Position control says 'go to this joint angle.' Force control says 'apply this much force.' Impedance control is the bridge: behave like a virtual spring-damper around a setpoint - the robot becomes mechanically compliant. Used everywhere humans and robots share workspace (cobots, surgical robots) and in any task involving contact (assembly, polishing, sanding). Admittance control is the dual: measure force, command position correction; works on stiff position-controlled robots with a wrist FT sensor.
bullets: Pure position control + stiff joints → robot punches the table when 1mm off
Impedance control: command torque to mimic K(x_des − x) − D ẋ; needs torque-controlled motors
Admittance control: measure force, command position correction; works on stiff robots + FT sensor
Hybrid force / position: split DOFs (x/y position-controlled, z force-controlled). Useful for wiping, polishing
Cartesian impedance - set stiffness in task space; rotational + translational stiffness diagonal
Practical tuning: low stiffness for safety, increase when tracking accuracy matters more

sections.heading: Motion planning algorithms
sections.body: Planning algorithms split into two families. Sampling-based planners (RRT, RRT*, PRM) randomly sample configurations and connect them; complete in the limit but produce jerky paths. Optimization-based planners (CHOMP, STOMP, TrajOpt) take a seed trajectory and refine it for smoothness and clearance; faster + smoother but can stick in local minima. The widget below shows RRT growing a tree toward the goal - drag the goal-bias slider to see how the tree shape changes.
bullets: RRT-Connect - fast default in MoveIt; bidirectional tree from start + goal
RRT* - asymptotically optimal; slower; great when you can wait a few seconds
PRM - pre-computed roadmap; query-time fast; needs a roadmap-build phase
CHOMP - gradient-based smoothing; differentiable; can stick in local minima
STOMP - stochastic gradient; robust to non-differentiable costs
TrajOpt - sequential convex programming; handles obstacles + dynamics jointly
Hybrid: RRT to find a feasible path, then optimization to smooth it

sections.heading: Trajectory generation + time parameterization
sections.body: A motion planner produces a path (sequence of configurations). To execute it on hardware you need a trajectory: a path with timestamps that respects velocity, acceleration, and torque limits. Polynomial parameterization (cubic, quintic spline through the waypoints) is fastest. TOPP-RA (Time-Optimal Path Parameterization based on Reachability Analysis) computes the fastest valid timing in polynomial time and is the modern default - MoveIt 2 ships it as a plugin.
bullets: Path = where to go. Trajectory = where to go + when
Polynomial parameterization - quintic splines through waypoints; smooth jerk
TOPP-RA - convex, fast, optimal under linear constraints (vmax, amax, torques)
Iterative parabolic time parameterization - MoveIt's older default; less optimal than TOPP-RA
Cartesian-space vs joint-space parameterization - pick by task (linear move = Cartesian)
ros2_control's joint_trajectory_controller takes a parameterized trajectory and runs it

sections.heading: ros2_control - the controller framework
sections.body: ros2_control is the standard layer between hardware drivers and high-level planners (MoveIt, Nav2). It provides a real-time deterministic control loop, a plugin system for hardware interfaces (CAN, EtherCAT, USB), and a controller manager that loads / unloads controllers (joint_trajectory, diff_drive, force_torque, admittance). If you build your own arm, mobile platform, or AMR, you almost certainly want ros2_control under the hood.
bullets: Hardware interface plugin - talks to the actual motor drivers
Resource manager - tracks state interfaces (position, velocity, effort) per joint
Controllers: joint_trajectory_controller, joint_position_controller, joint_effort_controller, diff_drive_controller, ackermann_steering_controller, admittance_controller
Real-time - runs in a SCHED_FIFO thread. No Python in the hot loop, only C++
Controller spawner - load/unload controllers at runtime without restarting the robot stack
Test on a fake hardware before real bring-up - mock_components is your friend

codeBlocks.title: List + spawn a controller at runtime
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 control list_hardware_interfaces
ros2 control list_controllers
ros2 control load_controller --set-state active joint_trajectory_controller

codeBlocks.title: Position controller with PID gains

sections.heading: Real-time control - when timing actually matters
sections.body: Generic Linux gives you ~1 ms of scheduling jitter - fine for perception (10 Hz), terrible for an attitude controller (1000 Hz). Hard real-time means a deadline that MUST be met, not should. The standard fix on Linux is the PREEMPT_RT patchset, which makes most kernel threads preemptable and gives you ~50 µs jitter. For very tight loops (motor commutation, FOC), put the loop on a microcontroller (STM32, RP2040) and bridge to ROS via micro-ROS or a custom protocol.
bullets: Stock Linux: ~1 ms scheduling jitter, occasional 10s of ms outliers
PREEMPT_RT: ~50 µs jitter, p99 in the 100-200 µs range
Loop pinning: chrt -f 80 + isolcpus + cpu affinity = exclusive CPU core for the controller
Avoid in the hot loop: malloc / new, exceptions, syscalls (besides timer / read), virtual calls
Pre-allocate everything. Use std::array, fixed-size containers, object pools
Microcontroller: STM32 / RP2040 for kHz+ loops; STM32 timer-encoder mode for free encoder decoding
micro-ROS bridges your MCU to a ROS 2 host over serial / Ethernet / CAN

codeBlocks.title: Confirm a real-time kernel is loaded
codeBlocks.code:
uname -a       # → ...PREEMPT_RT...
uname -v       # → SMP PREEMPT_RT ...
# Check thread is running with SCHED_FIFO
chrt -p $(pidof my_control_node)
